retrieved_docs.title: Hypotonic Labor -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Palpation and monitoring tools like an external tocodynamometer can be used to qualitatively evaluate uterine activity. This assessment is subjective since the diagnosis of hypocontractility is based on the perception that contractions are infrequent and weak, with less than 3 to 4 contractions/10 minutes and duration of less than 45 seconds. Another qualitative evaluation of progression is the cervical assessment by digital examination. This tracks the changes in cervical dilatation, effacement, and fetal station performed at standard timed intervals from admission time through the various stages of labor. These changes may be charted on a partogram, which aids in comparison with the expected lower limit of normal labor progress. A deviation to the right of the normal labor curve on the partograph denotes slow progress. [7] A protraction in the active phase would refer to cervical dilation slower than 1 to 2 cm/hour, whereas active phase arrest is diagnosed where there is no cervical change for >6 hours with inadequate contractions. [8] The internal pressure catheter is a device that measures the pressure generated by each contraction, recorded quantitatively in Montevideo units (MVU). Uterine activity less than 200 to 250 MVU is considered inadequate and unlikely to affect expected cervical dilation and fetal descent. This may be a more objective assessment than external monitoring in very obese women.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cervical Dilation -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: One meta-analysis suggested that ultrasound may play a new role in measuring cervical dilation to assess labor progression accurately. Still, at the moment, the digital cervical exam remains the gold standard. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cervical Dilation -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The most commonly used method of measuring cervical dilation during labor is a digital cervical exam. The obstetrician places two fingers inside the vagina and measures the width of the opening of the external os of the cervix using fingers to approximate the distance. In simple terms, 1 cm is about one finger's width, and 3 cm is the width of two fingers. 4 cm dilated is when the two fingers are approximately 1 cm apart. The widest stretch of the two fingers is about 7 to 8 cm. At 10 cm (full dilation), the cervix should not be present in front of the presenting fetal part (typically the head).

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Insufficiency -- Evaluation -- Ultrasonography
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical insufficiency is a well-recognized cause of second-trimester fetal loss and early preterm birth, and the diagnosis is often made retrospectively. Therefore, multiple professional guidelines recommend that individuals with a prior spontaneous preterm birth should be screened with serial cervical lengths in the second trimester. [1] [2] [13] [15] Screening is typically performed via transvaginal sonography every 1 to 2 weeks between 16 and 24 weeks gestation. Some clinicians also assess the cervical length in the first trimester, typically between 11 and 14 weeks gestation. A shortened cervix is typically defined as a cervical length of less than 25 mm in patients undergoing serial screening ultrasounds. [1] [2] [13] [15] Funneling, which refers to a ballooning of the fetal membranes into the cervical canal, and debris within the amniotic fluid may also be seen as the cervix shortens.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cervical Dilation -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: A pelvic scoring system, known as the Bishop score, is an important criterion utilized to determine the probability of successful labor induction. It is a clinical tool used to assess the cervix in pregnant women that can be obtained at the time of induction by a digital cervical exam. This scoring system considers the dilation and effacement of the cervix, its position and consistency, and the fetal station. There is a maximum score of 13, with a higher score (defined as 6+) indicating a more "favorable" cervix for vaginal delivery. A lower score indicates a more "unfavorable" cervix for vaginal delivery and a higher chance of the woman undergoing cesarean delivery. [15]

retrieved_docs.content: PTB = preterm birth; CL = cervical length

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: PTL is diagnosed clinically based on symptoms and physical findings. ACOG's Practice Bulletin on the management of PTL states that the diagnosis of PTL is "generally based on clinical criteria of regular uterine contractions accompanied by a change in cervical dilation, effacement, or both, or initial presentation with regular contractions and cervical dilation of at least 2 cm." [1] The threshold for defining "regular contractions" has varied in the literature, ranging from 4 to 12 contractions per hour. [2] ACOG does not include a threshold in their definition, though the WAPM-PMF joint guideline recommends requiring 6 or more contractions per 30 minutes. [1] [2] WAPM-PMF recommends observing patients for at least 2 hours to assess contraction frequency and cervical change.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypotonic Labor -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The duration of each contraction also increases from 30 seconds in early labor to between 60 to 90 seconds in the latter part of labor. [5] Friedman established the accepted pattern for the normal progression of labor in the 1950s. The World Health Organization adopted the Friedman curve on the partograph, a graphical labor monitoring tool. Based on his data on nulliparous and multiparous women, he described that the transition from the latent to the active phase of labor occurs at 3 to 4 cm cervical dilatation. He also described that for most women, the minimum rate of cervical dilatation in the active phase is 1.2 cm/hour in nulliparous and faster in multiparous women at 1.5 cm cervical dilatation/hour.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cervical Dilation -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Failure of the cervix to dilate an appropriate amount in a certain amount of time can lead to protracted or arrested labor. The definitions of what is termed protracted vs. arrested labor are listed below. Abnormal labor patterns First stage Protracted latent phase (dilation < 6 cm) > 20 hours (primigravida) > 14 hours (multigravida) Protracted active phase (dilation 6+ cm) Dilation progresses less than 1 cm/hour Arrested active phase No cervical change for 4+ hours with adequate contractions No cervical change for 6+ hours with inadequate contractions

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Prehospital Deliveries -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Cervix
retrieved_docs.content: The cervix is assessed on a vaginal exam using a sterile technique to monitor labor progress during labor. Cervical dilation is measured in centimeters through digital examination with 2 fingers. Full dilation is typically defined as reaching a dilation of 10 centimeters. Effacement is measured as a percentage of thinning; a cervix that is 100% effaced refers to a cervix that has become paper-thin. [9] In active labor, before full dilation and effacement, the cervix can be felt as a rim of tissue on top of the infant's presenting body part, usually the head. When the cervix is fully dilated and effaced, it falls behind the fetal presenting part and is no longer palpable. Delivery may be imminent if the cervix is no longer palpable and the fetal head can be seen at the vaginal introitus. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Labor -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Labor begins with regular uterine contractions, resulting in cervical effacement and dilation. An essential piece of the patient history to obtain is the start time of contractions and their occurrence interval. An abdominal examination is a critical component of an obstetric exam as it provides an estimated fetal weight and an assessment of fetal presentation. The continuous monitoring of uterine activity discerns how frequent contractions are occurring. Internal pressure catheter monitoring measures the actual strength of uterine contractions. Fetal heart rate tracing provides reassurance of fetal well-being throughout the labor process.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- History and Physical -- Pelvic Examination
retrieved_docs.content: After ruling out placenta previa—via chart review or bedside ultrasound—and prelabor rupture of membranes, a sterile digital examination is typically performed to assess cervical dilation, effacement, and station. Cervical dilation is measured by locating the cervical os, spreading the fingers in a "V" shape, and estimating the distance in cm between the 2 fingers at the level of the internal cervical os. Effacement refers to cervical thinning and is assessed by estimating the percentage of remaining cervical length compared to the uneffaced cervix, which is typically about 4 cm in length. Station represents fetal descent and is assessed by determining the position of the leading fetal part relative to the maternal ischial spines. When the bony fetal presenting part aligns with the maternal ischial spine, the fetus is at 0 station. Stations proximal to the ischial spines range from -1 to -5 cm, while stations distal to the ischial spines range from +1 to +5 cm.

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Labor -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The stages and phases of labor have specific time intervals used in labor evaluation and interpretation. Ideally, patients are evaluated with or without pelvic examinations every 2 hours to assess labor progress. First Stage: 0 to 10 cm dilation Latent phase: 0 to 5 cm dilation Active phase: 6 cm dilation to complete cervical dilation Second Stage: uterine contractions along with maternal expulsive efforts leading to the descent of the presenting fetal part and delivery of the fetus Third Stage: time between delivery of the fetus and placental delivery

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- History and Physical -- Physical Examination
retrieved_docs.content: Digital pelvic examination: After ruling out placenta previa and membrane rupture, a digital exam is typically performed to better assess cervical dilation, effacement, and station. If cervical dilatation is noted to be at least 3 cm before 34 weeks EGA, the patient is highly likely to deliver preterm. The placental location must be determined by chart review or bedside ultrasound before performing a digital cervical exam.

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Labor -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Digital vaginal exams, conducted at different intervals throughout labor, assess maternal pelvimetry, bony pelvis shape, and capacity, as well as cervical effacement and dilation. Serial digital exams are used to assess the fetal position, station, and descent of the presenting part, which is crucial in determining the normal or abnormal progression of labor. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cervical Dilation -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The latent phase should not exceed 20 hours in a primigravida female or 14 hours in a multigravida female. If the mother does not fully dilate by these average time estimates, she is said to be having an arrest of the active phase of Stage 1 labor.

retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester, is mapped to short cervical length in pregnancy, indicating that the condition of cervical shortening at any stage of gestation is understood as a short cervical length—a key obstetric measurement used to evaluate the risk of preterm birth and guide patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2539
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, second trimester, and cervical shortening, third trimester, are both classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that shortening of the cervix at either stage of pregnancy signals a heightened risk of labor onset before term and warrants close monitoring and appropriate management to address threatened labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in the third trimester is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that this cervical change is recognized as a sign of potential onset of labor before term.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, second trimester, is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that this cervical change occurring in the second trimester is considered a sign of early or threatened labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1156
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized under the broader group of other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother and is recognized as an obstetric abnormality of the cervix. This condition encompasses three specific subtypes: Cervical shortening, second trimester; Cervical shortening, third trimester; and Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester. Each of these subtypes is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that a shortened cervical length at any stage of pregnancy can herald the onset of labor before term. The unspecified‑trimester subtype is also directly mapped to the concept of short cervical length in pregnancy, reinforcing its clinical definition. In addition to cervical shortening, Stenosis of cervix in pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is another obstetric cervical abnormality, illustrating that multiple cervical pathologies can arise during gestation and the peripartum period. Together, these entities highlight the importance of assessing cervical length and integrity throughout pregnancy, as alterations such as shortening or stenosis are linked to the risk of premature or threatened labor and require careful management to optimize maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2010
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized under other complications of birth and puerperium affecting management of the mother, indicating its relevance to maternal care during and after delivery. This condition encompasses two specific forms: cervical shortening in the second trimester and cervical shortening in the third trimester. Both the second‑trimester and third‑trimester cervical shortening are classified as early or threatened labor, underscoring that cervical shortening at any stage of pregnancy serves as a warning sign for potential premature labor and requires careful monitoring and management to protect maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1692
retrieved_docs.content: Cerclage (procedure) is clinically associated with cervical insufficiency (قصور عنق الرحم), indicating that this surgical technique is used to support a weakened cervix and prevent premature dilation in patients with this condition. In addition, optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is also clinically associated with cervical insufficiency, reflecting that pregnancy termination may be considered as a management option in severe cases where the insufficiency poses significant risk to maternal or fetal health. Both interventions are relevant clinical considerations in the assessment and treatment of cervical insufficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 993
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as an obstetric abnormality of the cervix and is considered one of the “other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother.” This condition is further divided into three trimester‑specific forms—Cervical shortening, second trimester; Cervical shortening, third trimester; and Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester. Each of these trimester‑specific forms is classified as early or threatened labor, a clinical presentation that is also referred to by the Arabic term مخاض كاذب (false labor). The unspecified‑trimester form of cervical shortening is directly mapped to the concept of short cervical length in pregnancy, emphasizing its measurement aspect. In addition to cervical shortening, stenosis of the cervix in pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is also listed under the broader category of obstetric cervical abnormalities, highlighting a related but distinct cervical pathology. Together, these relationships delineate how various forms of cervical shortening and stenosis are organized within obstetric complications and how they relate to early or threatened labor presentations, informing clinical assessment and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
retrieved_docs.content: Induction by cervical dilation and other procedures to assist delivery are closely related interventions used to facilitate childbirth, and they may be considered synonymous in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2063
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in the second trimester is classified as early or threatened labor, and early or threatened labor is further identified as “مخاض كاذب,” which translates to false labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2430
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in the third trimester is classified as early or threatened labor, and early or threatened labor is further identified as مخاض كاذب (false labor). This indicates that cervical shortening late in pregnancy is recognized as a sign of a threatened labor episode, which is commonly understood to represent a form of false labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: aligning it with other cervical structural abnormalities that can affect pregnancy outcomes. **Cervical shortening in pregnancy** is placed under “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium” and also belongs to the broader class of **abnormalities of cervix (obstetric)**. It is subdivided into trimester‑specific forms—**Cervical shortening, second trimester**, **Cervical shortening, third trimester**, and **Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester**—each of which is mapped to **Early or threatened labor**, indicating that a shortened cervical length in any trimester serves as a clinical marker for premature or threatened delivery. The unspecified‑trimester form is additionally linked to the concept **Short cervical length in pregnancy**, reinforcing its role as a measurable obstetric parameter. The “Other conditions or status of the mother” category also includes findings such as **Uterine size for dates discrepancy** and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1447
retrieved_docs.content: Cerclage (procedure) and REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR are both clinically associated with قصور عنق الرحم. قصور عنق الرحم, also known as cervical insufficiency, is a condition in which the cervix is unable to maintain its structural integrity during pregnancy, increasing the risk of premature dilation and preterm delivery. The cerclage procedure involves placing a suturing stitch around the cervix to reinforce it and prevent premature opening, thereby directly addressing the mechanical weakness characteristic of قصور عنق الرحم. Additionally, a poor reproductive history—such as prior miscarriages, preterm births, or other adverse pregnancy outcomes—has been linked to a higher likelihood of developing قصور عنق الرحم, indicating that past obstetric complications can serve as a clinical indicator for this cervical disorder. Recognizing both the therapeutic role of cerclage and the predictive value of reproductive history is essential for early identification and management of patients at risk for قصور عنق الرحم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2397
retrieved_docs.content: Dilatation of cervix for delivery is a specific technique that falls under the broader category of cervical dilation or incision used as a delivery aid, representing a particular method of facilitating childbirth by widening the cervix.
